content: For a controllable canvas game: define state first, expose it on window, wire input to a single keys/pressed object, update movement with capped dt, draw layers in order, then add restart and game-over. Keep input/update/draw reading the same state.

For a turn-based board game: store board[r][c], render row/col only at draw boundaries, implement select then legal-target commit, block input while animating/AI thinking, and expose a small game API for probes.

For an asset-backed animated game: request base sprites early, load/decode them before first draw, use drawImage for generated entities, track animation timers in state, and provide procedural fallbacks only when asset generation is unavailable.

For a tile-based grid game where the player navigates corridors and cannot pass through walls: define state.maze[row][col] at build time (0=corridor, 1=wall, 2=door, 3=exit). Position the player by tile coords (state.player.tx, ty); derive pixel coords (px=tx*TILE_SIZE) at draw time. Movement: on keydown, check maze[nextTy][nextTx] !== 1 BEFORE applying. Draw: nested loop over the maze for walls/corridors, then sprites on top. Probes: maze rendered (canvas non-blank), player not in wall (the canvas-grid-navigation recipe's auto_probe checks this), keydown moves player by exactly 1 tile.

For a side-scrolling platformer: state.platforms[] with {x, y, w, h} entries; state.player = {x, y, vx, vy, w, h, onGround}. Gravity each frame: player.vy += GRAVITY (e.g., 0.5); player.y += player.vy. Collision AFTER movement: iterate platforms; if player overlaps a platform AND was moving down (vy > 0), snap player.y = platform.y - player.h; player.vy = 0; player.onGround = true. Jump on Space: IF player.onGround THEN player.vy = -JUMP_POWER (e.g., -10). Camera-follow: subtract (player.x - canvas.width/2) from every draw x. Probes: gravity works (vy > 0 after a frame in air), can jump (player.y decreases after Space), can land (onGround flips false→true).

For a 3D first-person game: STRONGLY PREFER three.js via CDN over hand-rolling a raycaster — THREE.WebGLRenderer + Scene + Camera + Mesh ships real 3D in <500 lines. State: state.player = {x, y, z, yaw, pitch}; camera.rotation.order = 'YXZ'. Input: pointer-lock on canvas click; mouse moves yaw/pitch; ArrowUp moves forward along yaw vector (sin(yaw), 0, -cos(yaw)). PICK ONE convention for forward and use it identically for movement, billboards, lookAt — sign-flips between movement and camera produce 'walking opposite from facing' bugs (see playbook fps-camera-and-movement-vectors). Weapon view as screen-space overlay (parented to camera OR HTML ), NOT a world-space mesh. Probes: yaw exposed on window.state; pointer-lock requested on click; up-arrow changes player.x or .z (not both).

For a top-down action game (camera looks straight down): state.player = {x, y, facing} where facing is an angle in radians (or one of N/E/S/W). Free 8-direction movement: on keydown set vx/vy per pressed key; normalize diagonals (vx /= sqrt(2), same for vy). Enemies as objects {x, y, behavior}; simple AI = step toward player each tick. Projectiles spawn at player position with velocity matching facing. Probes: arrow-key movement changes player.x or .y; collision detected between projectile and enemy (AABB or circle-overlap).

For a grid puzzle game: state.board[row][col] as 2D array of cell values (0=empty or a piece type). Tetris-shape: piece position + rotation + shape matrix; on tick, attempt to move down; on collision, freeze + scan full rows for clear. 2048-shape: shift rows in input direction, merge adjacent equal values once per move, spawn a new tile at a random empty cell. Match-3 shape: after a swap, scan for 3+ in a row/col; clear matches; pieces above drop into gaps; cascade. Probes: board is 2D array on state; legal input mutates the board; score increases on clear/merge.

For a pseudo-3D road racer: state.road is an array of segments (z-distances) each with curve + hill values. Render bottom-up: project each segment to screen y via perspective formula, draw road quad. Player sprite stays centered at the bottom; horizon shifts left/right with curves. Steering: ArrowLeft/Right adjusts player.x relative to road center, clamped to road width. Speed: throttle adds to z; friction subtracts. Probes: state.player.speed > 0 after ArrowUp held; road segments visible (canvas non-blank); steering changes player.x.

For a paddle-ball game: state.ball = {x, y, vx, vy, r}; state.paddle = {x, y, w}; Breakout variant adds state.bricks[]. Each frame: ball.x += ball.vx; ball.y += ball.vy; bounce off walls (if x<0 || x>canvas.width then vx*=-1); bounce off paddle (if ball.y>paddle.y && ball.x within paddle.x range then vy*=-1); brick collision iterates bricks; on hit splice the brick + ball.vy*=-1. Paddle input: ArrowLeft/Right OR mouseX. Probes: ball moves (state.ball.x changes after a frame); ball bounces off walls; paddle responds to input.

For a lane-crossing game: state.lanes[] each with {direction, speed, obstacles[]}. Player advances toward target row by one lane per ArrowUp keypress. Each frame: advance all obstacles by lane.speed * lane.direction; wrap off-edge to opposite side. Collision: if any obstacle's bounds overlap player position → lose a life. Score: number of safe crossings. Probes: lanes rendered (canvas non-blank); player moves on Up (state.player.row decreases); obstacles cycle (state.lanes[i].obstacles[0].x changes over time).

For a point-and-click adventure: state.scenes (each with background + hotspots[] of {x, y, w, h, on_click}). Mouse click hit-tests against the active scene's hotspots; matched hotspot fires its on_click (changes scene, gives item, plays dialog). Use canvas.getBoundingClientRect() to map clientX/Y → canvas coords. Inventory: state.inventory[] of item ids; render as a HUD strip. Probes: clicking a hotspot fires a state change (state.scene changes OR state.inventory grows); cursor shape changes over hotspots.

For an isometric tile grid: store world as tiles[row][col] of type ids. Convert (col, row) → screen with iso projection: sx = (col - row) * TILE_W/2; sy = (col + row) * TILE_H/2. Render row-by-row from top so depth ordering is correct (later rows draw on top of earlier). Click handling: invert the iso projection — col = (sx/(TILE_W/2) + sy/(TILE_H/2))/2; row = (sy/(TILE_H/2) - sx/(TILE_W/2))/2. Probes: tiles is 2D array on state; clicking a tile sets state.selected to its (col, row).

For an overworld RPG: tile map for terrain; NPCs as objects {x, y, dialogue, behavior}; player movement on a grid (snap to tile each step, no in-between pixels). Talking: when player faces an NPC and presses Action, advance dialogue state (state.dialogue = {npc, line_index}). Battle / scene transitions: state.scene as string ('overworld' | 'battle' | 'menu'); switch the active draw routine on change. Probes: map rendered; arrow keys move player tile by tile (state.player.tx changes by 1); pressing Action near an NPC opens dialogue (state.dialogue.open === true).

For a vertical platformer with ladders: state.platforms[] with y-positions; state.ladders[] with {x, yTop, yBot} where yTop and yBot DERIVE from platform.y values (NOT hardcoded pixels — drift otherwise). Hazards (barrels, falling enemies) need a respawn LOOP: each frame, advance and remove off-screen entries; spawn a new one every N seconds. Climbing: when player.x is inside a ladder column AND a keyDown is held, ignore gravity and adjust y by climb speed. Probes: at least 2 platforms exist; ladders touch a platform above and a platform below (the canvas-vertical-platformer recipe's auto_probe checks this); hazards keep coming (state.totalSpawned > state.hazards.length over time).

For a 2-character fighting / dueling game: state.players = [p1, p2] each with {x, y, facing, state, hp}. INITIAL positions: p1 on the left half, p2 on the right half. Facing convention: left-player.facing = +1 (points right), right-player.facing = -1 (points left). Sprite draw: respect facing via a single ctx.scale(facing, 1) wrapper inside save/restore. If the user later asks 'flip just the punch direction' or similar, the MINIMUM change is to wrap ONLY that state's drawcall — do NOT restructure the facing state machine (see playbook scope-flip-minimal-change). Probes: both players visible; both players face each other (canvas-two-actors-facing has an auto_actors_face_each_other_strict probe); attack input causes a state change AND a visible attack pixel in front of the attacker.

For particle / VFX / fluid sandbox: state.particles[] of {x, y, vx, vy, life, color}. Each frame: update every particle (advance position, decay life), remove dead ones, spawn from emitters. Render with low alpha (ctx.globalAlpha=0.6) for blending; use additive composite (ctx.globalCompositeOperation='lighter') for fire/lasers. Cap particles.length to prevent FPS death (e.g., MAX_PARTICLES = 800; drop oldest when over). Probes: at least N particles exist after T seconds; particle count is stable (not growing unboundedly).

For a city / economy builder: tile-based world + resource pools (state.resources = {money, power, water, ...}) + buildings[] with {type, x, y, level, output}. Each tick: every building generates output and consumes inputs; insufficient inputs degrade output. Build menu: cursor + selected building type; click places if (affordable AND tile is free). Probes: resource counter updates per tick; placing a building costs resources; rendered tiles match the tiles[][] data.

For a space-trading / exploration game: state.systems[] of {id, x, y, market: {commodity → price}}. Player ship has {x, y, fuel, cargo, money}. Travel = navigate to a system (consumes fuel). Market: buy low / sell high; prices vary per system. Combat (optional): enemy ships with HP + simple chase-or-flee AI. Probes: at least N systems generated on init; navigating consumes fuel (state.player.fuel decreases); buying changes cargo AND money.
